nativeRange.habitat: Generally found in lagoons and outer reef slopes between roughly 3 and 20 m depth, where individuals associate closely with host anemones.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical Western Pacific reef; sheltered lagoons and outer reef edges with hard substrate hosting Heteractis crispa and Stichodactyla mertensii anemones.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs
host anemone optional (Heteractis crispa or Stichodactyla mertensii)

diet.notes: Generally accepts most prepared marine fare. A varied diet of frozen mysis, brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, marine pellet/flake foods and herbivore preparations is commonly recommended; small portions two to three times daily tend to work well.

avoidWith: other clownfish species in the same tank
predatory fish that may eat small clownfish
triggers and large angelfish that may bully them

summary: A peaceful orange-and-white anemonefish from the Western Pacific, generally considered one of the calmer clownfish; reef-safe and commonly hosted by Heteractis crispa and Stichodactyla mertensii.
careNotes: Amphiprion sandaracinos is widely regarded as one of the more peaceful clownfish species and is generally considered hardy enough for newer marine aquarists, though several retailers still rate it Moderate rather than Easy because it can be out-competed at feeding time by bolder tankmates. In the wild it associates almost exclusively with Heteractis crispa and Stichodactyla mertensii, but it does not require a host anemone in captivity and will often shelter in rockwork. Like all clownfish it is a protandrous (male-first) sequential hermaphrodite: in a bonded pair the larger fish is the female and the smaller is the functional male; sex change has been reported around 5.1 cm TL. Best kept singly or as a mated pair, as multiple individuals of this or any other clownfish species in the same tank tend to lead to lethal aggression (tank-bred conspecifics introduced together at a young age are the usual exception). Reported maximum sizes vary considerably: LiveAquaria lists 4 inches (~10 cm) while FishBase lists 14 cm TL, so the 9-12 cm band used here is a cross-source compromise leaning toward typical captive size. Temperature reports also vary (LiveAquaria 22.2-25.6°C, FishBase 27-29.3°C, hobby community guides 24-28°C); the 24-28°C band reflects the average of these sources. Temperament rating is similarly split (LiveAquaria 'semi-aggressive', most community sources 'peaceful'); 'peaceful' is used here as it matches the species' well-documented mild reputation, with conspecific aggression noted separately as high.
breedingNotes: Has been bred and reared in captivity. Egg-layer that deposits demersal eggs on a cleaned flat surface near the host anemone; the male guards and aerates the clutch, which generally hatches in roughly 6-11 days depending on temperature. Larvae require very small live foods (rotifers, then enriched newly-hatched brine shrimp) in a dedicated rearing tank. Generally reported as somewhat harder to spawn than ocellaris/percula but easier than perideraion.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary species product page located via the clownfish collection root. Source of care-level, tank-size, temperament, water-parameter, and reef-safety figures.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Allen, 1972), family (Pomacentridae), max size (14 cm TL), preferred wild temperature (27-29.3°C), Western Pacific distribution, protandrous hermaphroditism, length at sex change (5.1 cm TL), and host anemones (Heteractis crispa, Stichodactyla mertensii).

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply care guide
additional.notes: Third-source confirmation of 30 US gal minimum tank, 75-82°F temperature, 8.1-8.4 pH, 1.020-1.025 SG, peaceful temperament, omnivorous diet, and reef-safe behaviour.

additional.name: AquariumDomain species profile
additional.notes: Used to corroborate the peaceful temperament and reef-safe rating where LiveAquaria's 'semi-aggressive' tag disagreed with most other community sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive-bred at commercial scale (e.g., ORA) but generally reported as somewhat harder than ocellaris/percula; larvae need rotifer cultures and enriched brine shrimp in a dedicated rearing tank.
